---
title: "Interactive Visualization with R Shiny"
subtitle: "PLSC 498 - Week 15, Lecture 1"
author: "Jared Edgerton"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: [default, metropolis, metropolis-fonts, plsc498.css]
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      slideNumberFormat: ""
---

# What is R Shiny?

.pull-left[
## Interactive Web Applications

Shiny is a framework for building interactive web applications directly from R.

**Key Features:**
- No JavaScript knowledge required
- Reactive programming model
- Deploy to web servers
- Real-time interactivity
- Customizable UI/UX
]

.pull-right[
## Why Use Shiny?

- **Share insights** without requiring R knowledge
- **Exploratory data analysis** with students/collaborators
- **Publication-quality** dashboards
- **Educational tool** for visualization
- **Rapid prototyping** of interactive ideas
]

---

# Shiny Applications Structure

Every Shiny app has two key components:

```r
library(shiny)

# 1. UI (User Interface) - defines layout and controls
ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("My App"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      sliderInput("n", "Select n:", min=1, max=100, value=50)
    ),
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput("plot")
    )
  )
)

# 2. Server - defines reactive logic
server <- function(input, output) {
  output$plot <- renderPlot({
    plot(1:input$n, main = paste("Plot with", input$n, "points"))
  })
}

# 3. Run the app
shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)
```

---

# Understanding Reactive Programming

Shiny uses **reactive programming**: when inputs change, outputs automatically update.

```
User Input (slider, button, text)
    ↓
Shiny Detects Change
    ↓
Updates Reactive Expression
    ↓
Automatically Re-renders Output
    ↓
User Sees New Result
```

This happens **instantly** in the browser.

---

# Key Input Functions

| Function | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `textInput()` | Text box | `textInput("name", "Your name:")` |
| `numericInput()` | Number entry | `numericInput("age", "Age:", 25)` |
| `sliderInput()` | Slider bar | `sliderInput("n", "N:", 1, 100, 50)` |
| `selectInput()` | Dropdown | `selectInput("year", "Year:", 2020:2024)` |
| `checkboxInput()` | Single checkbox | `checkboxInput("agree", "I agree")` |
| `checkboxGroupInput()` | Multiple checkboxes | `checkboxGroupInput("parties", "Parties:", c("D","R"))` |
| `radioButtons()` | Radio buttons | `radioButtons("choice", "Pick one:", c("A","B"))` |
| `actionButton()` | Clickable button | `actionButton("go", "Run Analysis")` |
| `dateInput()` | Date picker | `dateInput("date", "Select date:")` |
| `fileInput()` | File upload | `fileInput("upload", "Choose file:")` |

---

# Key Output Functions

| Function | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| `textOutput()` | Text display | `textOutput("summary")` |
| `plotOutput()` | Plot display | `plotOutput("scatter")` |
| `tableOutput()` | Table (static) | `tableOutput("data")` |
| `dataTableOutput()` | Table (interactive) | `dataTableOutput("data")` |
| `htmlOutput()` | HTML content | `htmlOutput("info")` |
| `verbatimTextOutput()` | Code/text | `verbatimTextOutput("output")` |

**In server:**
```r
output$scatter <- renderPlot({ ggplot(...) })
output$summary <- renderText({ "Summary here" })
output$data <- renderTable({ my_data })
```

---

# Common UI Layouts

## Sidebar Layout (Most Common)
```r
sidebarLayout(
  sidebarPanel( /* controls */ ),
  mainPanel( /* outputs */ )
)
```

## Multiple Tabs
```r
tabsetPanel(
  tabPanel("Tab 1", /* content */),
  tabPanel("Tab 2", /* content */)
)
```

## Navbar (Navigation Bar)
```r
navbarPage(
  title = "App Name",
  tabPanel("Home", /* content */),
  tabPanel("About", /* content */)
)
```

## Column Layout
```r
fluidRow(
  column(6, /* left side */),
  column(6, /* right side */)
)
```

---

# Reactive Expressions

A **reactive expression** recalculates whenever its inputs change:

```r
server <- function(input, output) {

  # Define a reactive expression
  filtered_data <- reactive({
    data %>%
      filter(year >= input$year_min,
             year <= input$year_max,
             party %in% input$party_choice)
  })

  # Use the reactive expression in outputs
  output$plot <- renderPlot({
    ggplot(filtered_data(), aes(x = ideology, y = votes)) +
      geom_point()
  })

  output$table <- renderTable({
    filtered_data()
  })

  # Both outputs update when inputs change!
}
```

**Key Point:** `filtered_data()` is called like a function, not a variable.

---

# Senate Ideology App: Overview

We'll create an interactive app to explore US Senate voting patterns.

**Data:** US Senators' DW-NOMINATE ideology scores (liberal → conservative)

**Features:**
- Filter by Congress (time period)
- Filter by party
- Interactive scatter plot
- Summary statistics
- Data download option

---

# Senate App: Basic Structure

```r
library(shiny)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Load data
senate_data <- readRDS("senate_ideology.rds")

# UI
ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("US Senate Ideology"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput("congress", "Congress:",
                  choices = unique(senate_data$congress)),
      checkboxGroupInput("party", "Parties:",
                        choices = c("D", "R"), selected = c("D", "R"))
    ),
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput("plot"),
      tableOutput("summary")
    )
  )
)

# Server
server <- function(input, output) {
  filtered <- reactive({
    senate_data %>%
      filter(congress == input$congress,
             party %in% input$party)
  })

  output$plot <- renderPlot({
    ggplot(filtered(), aes(x = dwnom1, y = dwnom2, color = party)) +
      geom_point(size = 3) +
      scale_color_manual(values = c("D" = "blue", "R" = "red"))
  })

  output$summary <- renderTable({
    filtered() %>%
      group_by(party) %>%
      summarize(mean_ideology = mean(dwnom1))
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
```

---

# Running Your App

**In RStudio:**

1. Write your Shiny code in a file called `app.R`
2. Click the **"Run App"** button in the toolbar
3. A window opens with your interactive app
4. Stop the app by pressing Escape or clicking Stop

**From Command Line:**
```r
# In R console
setwd("path/to/app/directory")
shiny::runApp("app.R")
```

**Your browser opens automatically** and you can interact with the app.

---

# Reactive Flow Diagram

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│   User Inputs                   │
│  (sliders, dropdowns, buttons)  │
└──────────────┬──────────────────┘
               │
               ↓
      ┌────────────────┐
      │ Reactive Value │
      └────────┬───────┘
               │
               ↓
    ┌──────────────────────┐
    │ Reactive Expression  │
    │ (filtered_data())    │
    └──────────┬───────────┘
               │
         ┌─────┴─────┐
         ↓           ↓
    ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐
    │Output1 │  │Output2 │
    └────────┘  └────────┘
         │           │
         └─────┬─────┘
               ↓
        ┌─────────────┐
        │ Web Browser │
        └─────────────┘
```

---

# Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Forgetting parentheses on reactive:**
```r
# Wrong:
plot(filtered_data)  # filtered_data is a function!

# Correct:
plot(filtered_data())  # Call the function with ()
```

2. **Putting all code in server without reactives:**
```r
# Inefficient - recalculates everything
output$plot1 <- renderPlot({ data %>% filter(...) %>% plot() })
output$plot2 <- renderPlot({ data %>% filter(...) %>% summary() })

# Better - calculate once, reuse
filtered <- reactive({ data %>% filter(...) })
output$plot1 <- renderPlot({ plot(filtered()) })
output$plot2 <- renderPlot({ summary(filtered()) })
```

3. **Not loading required packages**
```r
# Easy to forget:
library(shiny)
library(dplyr)  # Need this for %>%
library(ggplot2)  # Need this for ggplot()
```

---

# Senate App: Walkthrough

The complete basic Senate app includes:

- **Sidebar controls:** Congress selector, party checkboxes, slider for N senators
- **Three output tabs:**
  - Scatter plot of ideology in 2D space
  - Histogram distribution
  - Interactive data table
- **Reactive filtering:** All outputs update together
- **Professional styling:** Customized colors, labels, themes

This approach is ideal for **simple, focused dashboards** with a few outputs.

---

# When to Use Basic Sidebar Layout

**Good for:**
- ✓ Small apps with few controls
- ✓ Single primary visualization
- ✓ Quick prototyping
- ✓ Teaching/learning Shiny

**Consider alternatives if:**
- ✗ Many different sections (use `navbarPage`)
- ✗ Multiple related analyses (use `tabsetPanel`)
- ✗ Dashboard-style layout (use `shinydashboard`)
- ✗ Modern appearance important (use `bslib`)

---

# Key Takeaways

1. **Shiny creates interactive web apps from R** - no web development needed
2. **Two components:** UI (layout) and Server (logic)
3. **Reactive programming:** inputs → reactives → outputs automatically
4. **Input/Output functions** provide the building blocks
5. **Sidebar layout** is simple and effective for basic apps
6. **Reactive expressions** avoid code duplication
7. **Always remember:** call reactives with parentheses `reactive()`

---

# Next Lecture Preview

Lecture 2 will cover:

- ✓ Advanced layouts (navbarPage, multiple tabs)
- ✓ Shinydashboard for professional dashboards
- ✓ bslib for modern theming
- ✓ COW interstate conflicts app walkthrough
- ✓ Deployment and sharing apps

---

# Resources

**Official Documentation:**
- [Shiny Official Site](https://shiny.rstudio.com/)
- [Shiny Cheat Sheet](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rstudio/cheatsheets/master/shiny.pdf)

**Tutorials:**
- [RStudio Shiny Tutorial](https://shiny.rstudio.com/tutorial/)
- [Shiny Gallery](https://shiny.rstudio.com/gallery/)

**Books:**
- "Mastering Shiny" by Hadley Wickham (free online)

**Our Course Apps:**
- `senate_app_01_basic.R` - Basic sidebar layout
- `senate_app_02_navbar.R` - Navigation bar layout
